AbbVie's Neuroscience Commitment for Mental Health Awareness

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Nicholas Donoghoe
1mo
  • Report this post

May is Mental Health Awareness Month, a meaningful reminder of the millions of people around the world living with mental health conditions, and of the ongoing need for new approaches to care. At AbbVie, our commitment to neuroscience is deeply rooted in the belief that understanding the complexities of the brain requires sustained research, strong collaboration and fresh thinking across discovery and development. I am encouraged by the recent progress and milestones across our neuroscience pipeline and by the partnerships that are helping us explore promising new pathways and advance potential treatment options. Behind every scientific advancement are patients and families hoping for better options and better outcomes. Beyond our work in the clinic, it is just as important that we are raising awareness and supporting the patient community. Learn more below about how we are working with Mental Health America to encourage conversations and reduce the stigma around mental health. 

AbbVie

2,035,065 followers

1mo

This #MentalHealthMonth, we need to talk. Conversations can help reduce stigma, and the words we choose matter. We’re partnering with Mental Health America to share tips on person-first language. Follow along this month to learn more.

    As part of Mental Health Awareness Week, we’re continuing to share the people behind our services.           #mentalhealthawarenessweek Here Joe Harkness talks with Chris Packham about birdwatching and how it relates to the Five Ways to Wellbeing. Five actions that can help with our mental health even in our darkest moments. Simple actions that can help to give us space, and time to recover. Nature can be our constant companion if we actively seek it out, connect, take notice, keep learning, and give back to it.

          Joe Harkness

            What an impactful way to convey a critical message. This short video conversation highlights the value of lived experience in supporting others who share a piece of that kind of knowing. While watching the birds they review ways to support wellbeing that can be applied to nature relationship building: connection, education, being active, giving, and noticing. They even talk about moving mindful nature connection practices indoors when outside spaces are not available/accessible. It reminds me of the many ways that knowing our place of belonging in nature saves us over and over. Thanks Joe and Chris!
